question.text: A company is NOT permitted to have more than one ISIN for its equity shares UNLESS which of the following applies?

answer.text: The shares have differential voting rights or different paid-up values
The company is listed on both BSE and NSE simultaneously
The company has more than 10 lakh shareholders
The company has issued both interim and final dividends in the same year

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A company is generally not permitted more than one ISIN for its equity shares. The exception is when shares carry differential voting rights or have different paid-up values -- these represent genuinely different classes of securities and require separate ISINs. **The Trap:** Being listed on multiple exchanges does NOT require separate ISINs -- the same ISIN is used across exchanges. Similarly, the number of shareholders or dividend history has no bearing on ISIN requirements. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series VI (Depository Operations) covers ISIN allotment in detail; Series II-A covers the RTA's role in ISIN management for corporate actions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When DVR (Differential Voting Rights) shares exist, ensure clients understand the difference -- DVR shares typically trade at a discount to ordinary shares due to lower voting rights but may offer higher dividends.
